title: Paris in 48 Hours
intro: Paris rewards the traveler who resists the urge to sprint between monuments and instead surrenders to a single arrondissement at a time. This weekend moves deliberately — from the medieval heart of the Île de la Cité and the cobblestoned pulse of Le Marais on Day 1, to the grand Left Bank sweep of Saint-Germain and the Invalides on Day 2. Two days, eight stops, and an embarrassment of centuries.

days.label: Day 1
days.subtitle: Île de la Cité, Le Marais & a Speakeasy Nightcap

blocks.title: Stained Glass at the Edge of the Possible
blocks.body: Arrive the moment the doors open, before tour groups dilute the silence, and climb to the upper chapel where 1,113 square feet of 13th-century stained glass dissolve the stone walls into pure, shimmering color. On a clear morning the rose window throws magenta and gold across the flagstones in a way that makes every photograph feel insufficient. Louis IX built this to house the Crown of Thorns; what he actually built was a reliquary of light.
blocks.tip: Book tickets online the night before — the queue outside moves slowly and eats into your morning.

blocks.title: Falafel, No Apologies
blocks.body: Cross the Pont Saint-Louis and walk northeast into the Marais to the rue des Rosiers, where L'As du Fallafel has been frying chickpea patties to crackling, cumin-scented perfection for decades. Order the special — eggplant, cabbage, hummus, amba — and eat it standing on the cobblestones like everyone else. The line looks intimidating and moves in twelve minutes.
blocks.tip: The shop is closed on Saturdays for Shabbat; this itinerary runs Friday–Sunday, so arrive Saturday before 3 p.m. or swap to Sunday.

blocks.title: Picasso in a Seventeenth-Century Townhouse
blocks.body: A short walk north through the Marais brings you to the Hôtel Salé, a limestone hôtel particulier whose courtyard alone justifies the detour. Inside, the chronological hang of Picasso's work — Blue Period melancholy giving way to Cubist geometry and then the late carnival of color — reads like a one-man history of modern art. Budget ninety minutes and don't skip the sculpture garden.
blocks.tip: The museum is free the first Sunday of each month, which is wonderful news and terrible crowd news — plan accordingly.

blocks.title: Provençal Warmth, Then a Tequila Secret
blocks.body: Two blocks from the Place des Vosges, Chez Janou packs cheerful Provençal cooking — ratatouille, pan-seared fish, a legendary chocolate mousse you serve yourself from a communal bowl — into a room humming with good-natured noise. After dinner, follow the Marais's nocturnal drift south to Candelaria, a taqueria whose back wall conceals one of the city's most inventive cocktail dens. Ring the buzzer and order whatever the bartender recommends.
blocks.tip: Chez Janou doesn't take reservations for parties under four; arrive at 7:45 p.m. to put your name on the list and have a pastis at the zinc bar while you wait.

days.label: Day 2
days.subtitle: Left Bank Grandeur, Gardens & a Classic Nightcap

blocks.title: Breakfast with the Ghosts of Saint-Germain
blocks.body: The Flore is expensive, the waiters are magnificently indifferent, and the café crème arrives in a little white pitcher with a square of dark chocolate balanced on the saucer — and none of that matters because Sartre wrote here and the banquettes still smell faintly of ambition. Order the tartine, open whatever you're reading, and linger until the second espresso is gone. This is the Left Bank operating exactly as advertised.
blocks.tip: Weekday mornings are calmer; if you're here on a Sunday, expect a wait but the people-watching on the boulevard compensates.

blocks.title: Monet's Oval Rooms, Hushed and Immense
blocks.body: A fifteen-minute walk east through the Tuileries delivers you to the Orangerie's two oval rooms, where Monet's Water Lilies panels curve around the viewer in an almost architectural embrace of soft blues, greens, and fading lavender. The light is calibrated through skylights to match the diffuse northern glow Monet originally designed for — standing at the center of either room, you understand why he called it his life's work. The Walther Collection downstairs, a survey of early-20th-century painting, is excellent and almost always uncrowded.

blocks.title: Rodin's Garden, Then Lunch Among Vegetables
blocks.body: Cross the Seine and walk south along the Rue de Varenne to the Musée Rodin, where The Thinker broods in a rose garden and The Gates of Hell command a courtyard wall with operatic intensity. The garden ticket is cheaper than full admission and sufficient for a Sunday stroll among the bronze figures. Afterward, push the budget and book the adjacent L'Arpège for lunch — Alain Passard's vegetable-driven cuisine, with produce pulled from his own farms, is one of the most singular dining experiences in a city that does not lack for them.
blocks.tip: L'Arpège's lunch tasting menu is considerably less ruinous than dinner; reserve weeks in advance and dress as though you mean it.

blocks.title: The Tower at Dusk, Then Hemingway's Martini
blocks.body: Walk west along the Seine as the sky bruises violet and gold above the Champ de Mars — the Eiffel Tower at dusk earns every cliché ever written about it, the ironwork catching the last light before the hourly sparkle begins. Ride the elevator to the second floor for a view that recalibrates your sense of Paris's scale: the grid of Haussmann boulevards, the silver thread of the river, the dome of Sacré-Cœur blinking white on the northern hill. End the weekend properly at Bar Hemingway inside the Ritz, where the barman shakes a Serendipity as though the twenties never ended.
blocks.tip: The Eiffel Tower sparkles for five minutes at the top of every hour after dark — position yourself on the Trocadéro esplanade for the full effect before heading to the Ritz.
